records.text: Composition-with-Anthropic-skills decision: Dendrite skills compose with Anthropic's native .claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md format rather than competing with it. Native skills are the always-loaded floor (use for universally-relevant project orientation and core conventions). Dendrite skills are the dynamic, recall-scored layer on top (use for skills scoped to specific work patterns; emerge from repeated memories). The installer-shipped .claude/skills/dendrite-wiki/SKILL.md instructs the agent on how to discover Dendrite skills via wiki_context.skills and wiki_skill_load. This avoids being squashed by future Anthropic skill registry features and positions Dendrite as infrastructure those skills read from.

records.text: For dynamic indicators on VitePress nav links (e.g. notification counts on `Inbox`/`Review Board`), use Vue Teleport from a host component mounted in `nav-bar-content-after`. Pattern in `docs/.vitepress/theme/components/InboxNavBadge.vue`: (1) keep host component in the slot to own SSE/polling lifecycle; (2) on mount, querySelectorAll matching link elements (`a.VPNavBarMenuLink, a.VPNavScreenMenuLink` to cover BOTH the desktop nav and the mobile screen menu — they use different VPLink subclasses); (3) Teleport a `<span>` badge into each matched link; (4) attach a MutationObserver to `.VPNav` (NOT `.VPNavBar` — the mobile screen menu lives outside `.VPNavBar`) to refresh targets when VitePress re-renders the menu, but use a reference-equality check on the matched-list to skip no-op updates so the badge teleport (which itself mutates the link) doesn't loop. Avoid hardcoding base path: filter by `href` ending with `/wiki/...` so any `vitepress base` config works. Bonus: this approach naturally drops the standalone badge UI — the indicator now sits directly on the link the user needs to click, which is better UX (one visual cue, not two).

Handoff summary: Redesigned the Benchmark Report page (docs/.vitepress/theme/components/BenchmarkReport.vue) to match the MaintenanceReviewBoard's minimal-tactical aesthetic and added rich self-explaining hover tooltips to every data point. Two passes shipped this session: (1) visual rebuild — replaced gradient/shadow card panels with hairline-bordered sections, a stripe-tape "BENCHMARK" header, italic Times-serif `*Local*Benchmark` hero, a 4-stat strip with thin colored ticks, and 4-column trend rows (label · value · 32px sparkline · delta) with hairline dividers and a leading 2px tone-tick; (2) tooltip pass — every trend row, hero stat, status pill, hero-window dt/dd, recall strip cell, maintenance strip cell, and section header now carries a multi-line `title` describing what it measures, direction-of-good, latest value, and per-snapshot history series. Hover affordance via `cursor: help` and subtle text-1 3% background tint. New CSS tokens scoped under `.bm-report` (bm-color-improve/warn/orientation/health/recall/context/ops + bm-hairline). Script logic essentially unchanged — TrendMetric gained a required `description` field, three lookup tables (headlineMetricInfo, recallStripInfo, maintenanceStripInfo) describe the static stats, helpers (metricTooltip, headlineTooltip, stripCellTooltip, metricSeriesText, toneDirectionText) build the multi-line title strings; trendPath gained an optional height arg; shortCommit + statusLabel computeds added. tsc clean; verified end-to-end via agent-browser at 1400×2400 with DOM query confirming 15 trend rows + 4 stats + 10 strip cells all carry tooltips.

Next steps:
- Consider replacing native `title` tooltips with a custom component (e.g., a small popover that appears immediately on hover) if the OS-level ~1s delay feels too slow — review board pattern uses native title so consistency is fine for now.
- Dogfood the redesign for a session or two and watch whether the operator still has to ask 'what does X mean' — if a specific tooltip is unclear, refine that one description in the metric arrays / lookup tables.
- Evaluate whether the same tooltip pattern should be ported to MaintenanceReviewBoard.vue — its hero stat tiles and work-item rows could benefit from the same self-explanatory affordance.
- If recall benchmark history grows past ~12 snapshots, the truncated history line in metricSeriesText (`first → … → last 6`) may want re-tuning — current truncation triggers above 8 entries.

Open questions:
- Native title attribute multi-line rendering varies by browser — confirmed working in Chrome/Edge via DOM query, but Firefox/Safari behavior wasn't tested. Worth a manual cross-browser pass before assuming the tooltip pattern is universal.
- Should the section-header tooltip also include a short example (e.g., what a 'good' Wiki Health row looks like) — or is the per-row tooltip enough?
- The shadow-mode semantic recall strip currently has no tooltips on its three cells (Probes with cosine, Avg cosine across candidates, Avg cosine deterministic top-1). Worth adding for parity, or leave it sparse since it's a kill-switch metric most readers shouldn't care about?

records.text:
Handoff summary: Review Board row removal now sweeps off to the right before the gap closes, instead of disappearing instantly. The fix is purely CSS in MaintenanceReviewBoard.vue: (1) replaced the existing subtle `.rb-item-leave-*` rules with a staged sweep — translateX(112%) over 520ms with an aggressive ease-in tail, opacity fades 80ms in, and max-height/margin/padding/border-width collapse is delayed ~280ms so the row visibly leaves before the gap closes; (2) added `overflow-x: hidden` to `.work-list` so the off-screen translate doesn't trigger a page-level horizontal scrollbar; (3) **critical follow-up** — the first attempt silently no-op'd because `.work-item.just-completed` (specificity 0,2,0) sets a `transition` shorthand and stays on the row during the leave (the just-completed timer clears at 5.5s, long after the leave finishes), overriding the bare-class `.rb-item-leave-active` (0,1,0). Bumped leave/enter/move/leave-from/leave-to/enter-from selectors to `.work-item.rb-item-*` to tie specificity, and source order does the rest. Reduced-motion override at line ~2826 still uses bare-class selectors with `!important` and was left untouched (intentional — !important beats specificity, so reduced-motion users still get instant removal).

User reported "doesn't seem to work" after the first round; the specificity fix landed in a follow-up. User did not confirm whether the second attempt now visibly sweeps — they may still need a hard refresh (Ctrl+Shift+R) to bust HMR/CSS cache. No code-level testing was done; this is CSS-only and Vue's compiler accepts it without complaint.

Captured two memories: a skill on TransitionGroup leave staging (sweep first, then collapse the gap), and a warning on the specificity gotcha when sibling state classes set the `transition` shorthand during the leave.

Next steps:
- Confirm with the user that the sweep actually animates after a hard refresh — if it still snaps, inspect the leaving `<li>` in DevTools during the leave and check what `transition` resolves to (should be the long shorthand from .work-item.rb-item-leave-active). If the resolved transition is wrong, look for further specificity competitors not yet identified.
- Consider auditing other state classes in MaintenanceReviewBoard.vue that might set a `transition` shorthand and clash with TransitionGroup classes (search for `\.work-item\.[a-z-]+\s*\{` blocks that contain `transition:`). The just-completed clash was the obvious one — there could be more lurking for tone/category variants.
- If the sweep direction or speed feels off in user testing, tweak `.work-item.rb-item-leave-active` transform duration (currently 520ms) and easing (currently cubic-bezier(0.55, 0.06, 0.68, 0.19)). Decreasing duration to ~440ms or pushing translateX to 130% are easy levers.
- Run `npm run benchmark:snapshot -- --label session-end` if any further work happens in this area; this session was visual-only and no benchmark was captured at start either.

Open questions:
- Did the sweep land for the user after the specificity fix, or does it still feel like the item is teleporting? They reported the first attempt didn't work but did not return after the fix landed.
- Is the 520ms sweep duration the right length? It's intentionally longer than the previous 420ms to make the motion legible, but in fast workflows where the operator is clicking through many items rapidly, the row may still be mid-flight when they click the next action. Worth observing in real dogfood usage.
- Should the sweep direction be parameterized by purpose? Promote actions could sweep up-and-right (graduating), Quiet could sweep down (dismissing), Reconcile could sweep left (back into the wiki). Currently all three sweep right uniformly. Probably not worth doing unless the metaphor really helps comprehension — added complexity for marginal payoff.

records.text: Memory Trails design — three deterministic patterns ported from dendrite-mcp predecessor after audit revealed which bio-inspired patterns actually worked vs which silently failed: (1) Edge reinforcement + lazy evaporation: new project_memory_edges SQLite table with (from_kind, from_id, to_kind, to_id, edge_type, weight, last_reinforced_at, created_at, evaporation_rate). Reinforce on wiki_context recall hits (+0.05), wiki_skill_load (+0.10). LAZY on-demand evaporation at read time: effective_weight = weight * (1 - rate)^hours_since_reinforced. This sidesteps the predecessor's tokio-scheduler design (we're stdio MCP, no background process). Use as recall ranking bonus surfaced as 'reinforced Nx over last Nd' in reasons[]. Per-edge-type rates: query→memory 0.005/hr, memory→file 0.001/hr, page→page 0.0005/hr, attached_skill 0.003/hr. (2) LRU+TTL cache on wiki_context: 256 entries, 30-min TTL, invalidate on any wiki_write/memory_remember. Pure latency win. (3) Jaccard drift lint: tokenize wiki page front-matter intent vs last N project-log entries; Jaccard distance > 0.5 raises maintenance-review finding 'page drift suspected'. Works without embeddings or LLM. Predecessor's mycelial growth (embedding-based) is explicitly NOT ported because it ran broken for months in dendrite-mcp (smoking gun: store.rs:15167 comment 'name was a bug that silently disabled this pass for months').

records.text: Pattern for adding a new LanguageExtractor (Python is the worked example; Rust/Go follow the same shape): (1) detect() must require BOTH a project-signal file AND a usable toolchain — never claim a project whose extract() will throw at runtime. Memoize the toolchain lookup in module scope so detect() doesn't pay version-check overhead on every call. (2) For shell-out extractors, embed the helper script as a string constant and pipe it via stdin (`spawn(cmd, ['-', source-path])` for Python, equivalent for Ruby/etc.). NEVER pass the script via `-c` because Windows cmd.exe caps total command line at ~8KB. (3) The helper should emit JSON in the language-agnostic ApiFileReference shape directly — let the orchestrator do path resolution and slug derivation, the helper just produces symbols. (4) walk() should default to language-conventional excludes (Python: tests/, .venv/, build/, __pycache__/, site-packages/). Set respectInternalConvention:false for languages whose privacy convention isn't @internal JSDoc — the language-specific is_public() check should live in extract(). (5) Symbol-kind heuristics need at least one negative test case (e.g., DEFAULT_NAME != type-alias) — easy to write a heuristic that's right on PascalCase but wrong on SCREAMING_CASE. (6) Tests must skip cleanly when the toolchain isn't available on CI/dev — use `node:test`'s `{ skip: '...' }` option, not silent passing. Memoize-cache reset (`resetXyzInterpreterCache()`) is needed for tests that simulate "toolchain disappears."

records.text: Predecessor dendrite-mcp had a mycelial_growth_pass (store.rs:15148) that did O(n²) cosine similarity on embeddings to discover memory-to-memory connections. The audit revealed this pass ran BROKEN for months (the code looked for a 'memory_embeddings' table but the actual table was 'vec_items') and nobody noticed because there was no observable success metric. The lesson: bio-inspired patterns sound elegant in design docs but if they have no success metric they may be silently producing nothing — and you'll never know. When porting any pattern with statistical/probabilistic behavior, instrument it from day one (log empty-result rate, compare against a baseline, surface in benchmark snapshot). Specifically for this project: do NOT port mycelial growth or any embedding-dependent recall enhancement until we have (a) embeddings infrastructure and (b) a success metric in the recall benchmark that would catch silent failure.

records.text: Review Board design direction: the operator should not have to triage memories manually. Target shape is (1) a "Growing" tab showing active memories with no finding yet, (2) an "Auto-clean" button that delegates to the local LLM, which emits a structured {memoryId, verb, reason, confidence} per memory (verbs: archive, keep-and-watch, add-source, merge, promote-to-wiki, promote-to-skill, rephrase), (3) a summary panel that lets the operator audit each decision after the run. Confidence in the LLM comes from auditability + reversibility, not blind trust. Prerequisites: an unarchive/restoreMemory API (none exists today — archive is one-way) and a stable decision schema with per-run batch IDs so an entire auto-clean run is revertible. The whole feature fits inside the existing review→inbox→action architecture as a new judgment layer between "findings" and "actions"; no structural rewrite needed.

records.text: Skill enforcement decision: agents drift on tool discipline (documented in mem_7d531792). The skill discovery flow can't depend on agents remembering to call wiki_context. The fix is hook-injected enforcement: a UserPromptSubmit hook fires wiki_context automatically on every user prompt and injects matched skill summaries; a PreToolUse hook on Edit/Write fires a quick scope match against the file path/language and injects matching skill summaries as a system reminder. Hooks ship in the installer under .github/hooks/ alongside session-start, session-handoff, and benchmark hooks.

records.text: Skills matching approach decision: deterministic-only for v1 free tier, NO local LLM dependency. Rationale from auditing the predecessor dendrite-mcp project: (1) the LLM brought misfire rate from ~30% to ~5% but the bulk of the win came from deterministic guard-rails (language hard-filter + level/recency demotion), not the LLM; (2) our two-phase fetch design is fundamentally different — wiki_context returns skill summaries, the frontier coding agent (Claude/Cursor/etc) picks which to load via wiki_skill_load, so the frontier model already does semantic reranking and we don't need a second LLM; (3) dendritemcp-lessons explicitly lists 'Heavy Background Model Dependency' as a pattern to avoid. Borrowed deterministic guard-rails: scope hard-filters before scoring (conservative — missing scope dim keeps skill in candidates), recency demotion so historical high-recall skills don't dominate, token bigram bonuses for multi-word phrases. Optional Ollama/embedding reranker can be added post-S7 gated on real precision metrics if needed.

records.text: Skills-as-memory architecture decision: skills are a new memory kind ('skill') that extends the existing memory record with a scope schema (filePatterns, frameworks, languages, taskKeywords, matchMode). The frontier coding agent (Claude/Cursor/etc) — not a local LLM — picks which skills to use via a two-phase fetch: (1) wiki_context returns skill *summaries* matching the task scope; (2) the agent calls wiki_skill_load(id) for the ones it picks. This mirrors the existing wiki_search → wiki_read pattern and avoids requiring a local LLM. Free tier (no gating). Build phases S1–S7 in skills-as-memory.md.

records.text: Team-tier architecture decision: Team tier centers on a hosted node (Supabase + thin Node service initially) holding the canonical wiki/memory/skill store, plus a steward agent on that node that handles all cross-engineer merges. Sync is local-first: writes happen locally first, queue, then push in background. The steward classifies every action into high/medium/low confidence — high lands directly, medium lands with an auto-revert window, low queues for human review with the steward's recommendation attached. The reviewer always has final say. Recommended starting model for the steward is Claude API with prompt-cached system prompt (cost is fine at team scale, quality matters because errors create reviewer fatigue). records.text: Team-tier reporting model decision: pull-only, not push. The Team dashboard is a Next.js app managers open when they want to know status. No auto-post to Slack/email/etc. Rationale: (1) push requires connector infra (HubSpot/Slack/etc) which is explicitly out of scope per operator design call; (2) pull is cheaper to build (no webhook infra, no rate limits, no outbound auth flows); (3) an always-current dashboard solves the 'managers want status' problem without spamming chat. Future Friday-digest email could be added post-T7 if customer demand surfaces but is not on the initial roadmap. The product target: empower or eliminate the scrum-master role by removing engineer reporting overhead.

records.text: Two specific JSDoc/TSDoc gotchas that bit during the file-level docstring pass for the API reference generator: (1) Any literal `*/` sequence inside a JSDoc body terminates the block. This includes glob patterns like `**/*.ts` (which contains `**/*` → `*/` in the middle) — they look fine in source but the parser sees the comment end mid-pattern. Rewrite to avoid the literal `*/` substring, or break the pattern with whitespace. (2) When the API reference renderer extracts file-level JSDoc bodies into markdown pages, multi-line backtick spans containing `<word>` placeholders (like `dendrite-wiki skill:import\n<path>`) trip VitePress's Vue compiler — Vue parses the angle brackets as unclosed tags before markdown processing kicks in. Inline single-line backtick spans like `<id>` are usually fine, but anything that wraps to a new line breaks. Safe alternatives: rewrite as descriptive prose ("the subcommand takes a memory id"), or use square-bracket placeholders. Also: when a walker uses `/@internal\b/` to detect `@internal`-tagged files, anchor it to tag-position (`^[ \t]*\*?[ \t]*@internal\b` with /m) so prose mentions in docstrings don't cause modules to self-skip.

records.text: VitePress parses every markdown page as a Vue SFC, so any literal `<word>` substring in a page body trips the Vue tag parser with "Element is missing end tag" and breaks `npm run docs:build`. This is especially dangerous in auto-generated wiki pages that emit operator-supplied content into markdown — `docs/wiki/maintenance-inbox.md` is generated by `src/wiki/maintenance-inbox.ts` from project-local memory bodies, and a single memory containing `.github/agents/<name>.agent.md` was enough to break the whole docs build. The defense lives at the markdown sink, not the input: `escapeMarkdownForVue()` in `src/wiki/maintenance-inbox.ts` HTML-escapes `<` and `>` to `<`/`>` before emitting `finding.summary` into the `####` heading and `record.text` into the blockquote. The escape preserves backticks, code blocks, and other markdown formatting; it only neutralizes the Vue tag parser. When adding any new emit point in the inbox generator (or any other generator that writes user-supplied content into a `.md` file under `docs/wiki/`), apply `escapeMarkdownForVue` to the user-supplied portion. Test/maintenance-inbox.test.ts has a regression test ("escapes angle brackets in memory summary and body so VitePress can render the page") asserting both the heading and blockquote escape correctly.

records.text: When a Node test spawns the CLI via `child_process.execFile` with a Windows `.cmd` shim (e.g., `npx.cmd`, the platform-specific `npxCommand`), pass `shell: true` in the options. Without it, `execFile` returns `EINVAL` because Windows cannot directly execute `.cmd` files without a shell to dispatch them. The MCP SDK's StdioClientTransport handles this internally so existing MCP tests work, but a plain execFile call does not. `shell: true` is harmless on POSIX where `npx` is a real executable. Saw this on the api-cli tests; spent ~5 minutes recognizing EINVAL is the canonical Windows-cmd-shim error.

records.text:
When a TransitionGroup leave animation feels like an item is "just disappearing" rather than physically being removed, look at whether the slide and the height collapse are running on the same timeline. If they are, the row's gap closes while it's still in place, so the eye reads it as a fade — not a sweep.

The fix: stage them. Make the transform run first (longer duration, bigger distance — translateX(110%+) is enough to clearly leave the visible area), and add a `transition-delay` to max-height/margin/padding so the gap only starts closing after the row has visibly moved. Use an aggressive ease-in for the slide (cubic-bezier(0.55, 0.06, 0.68, 0.19)-ish) so it accelerates as it goes — this reads as "swept", not "drifted".

Why: subtle translateX values (e.g., 0.75rem) plus simultaneous height collapse compress into ~400ms of overlapping motion that the operator perceives as a single fade-out. Operators reported items "just disappearing" even though there was a 360ms transition. Two staggered phases (sweep, then collapse) each get their own perceptual moment.

How to apply: any list with TransitionGroup leave where you want the user to register affirmative removal. Also remember to add `overflow-x: hidden` (or `clip`) to the list container so the off-screen translate doesn't trigger a page-level horizontal scrollbar, and keep the existing `prefers-reduced-motion` override pointing at the leave-active rule so reduced-motion users still get instant removal.

items.summary: Memory has no supporting sources: When adding/changing a Vue `<TransitionGroup>` leave (or enter) transition, check whether any *other* class on the sa...

records.text:
When adding/changing a Vue `<TransitionGroup>` leave (or enter) transition, check whether any *other* class on the same element sets the `transition` shorthand. The TransitionGroup leave classes (`.foo-leave-active`, etc.) have specificity 0,1,0; if a sibling state class like `.work-item.just-completed` (0,2,0) is also on the element during the leave AND sets `transition: <any-property> ...`, that rule wins and silently *overrides* the entire leave transition shorthand. The transform/opacity/max-height properties then SNAP to their leave-to values instead of animating, and the row appears to disappear instantly even though the leave-to styles are correct.

Concrete example from this project: MaintenanceReviewBoard had `.work-item.just-completed { transition: border-color 320ms ease, box-shadow 320ms ease; }`. The `just-completed` class stays on the row during the leave (the visual-feedback timer clears it at 5.5s, well after the leave finishes), so it overrode `.rb-item-leave-active`'s transition. The fix is to bump the leave selectors to match the state class's specificity — e.g. `.work-item.rb-item-leave-active` — so they tie on specificity and win on source order.

How to apply: any time you write or debug a TransitionGroup transition, audit OTHER class rules that may apply to the same element during the transition. If any of them set the `transition` shorthand, raise the leave/enter rule's specificity (double-class selector is the cleanest) so it wins. Symptom to recognize: leave-to styles are correct on inspection but the element snaps instead of animating.

records.text: When building a TypeScript declaration→markdown renderer, two specific polish targets matter for output quality: (1) For interfaces, just call `printNode(node)` and strip the leading `export ` modifier. Don't try to render `interface Foo` headers without the body — readers care about the member shape, which IS the interface. The TS printer handles indentation and member ordering cleanly. (2) For function parameter signatures, build the rendered text manually instead of using `printNode(parameter)`. Default values like `= {}` are implementation detail and add visual noise. Build as `${dotDotDot}${name}${question}${typeAnnotation}` to keep just the contractual parts: rest token, identifier, optional marker, type. Saw this when dogfooding the API reference generator on this repo's 37-module src/ tree — the unfiltered output was cluttered, the polished output is professional. Discovered during Phase A6 of the api-reference-roadmap.

records.text: When documenting a flow, system, request path, or state machine in a wiki page, consider calling `wiki_insert_chart` to add a Mermaid diagram. The tool validates the source, anchors by heading (so the chart stays attached to the right section even after edits), and writes idempotently — calling it twice with the same source is a no-op. Anchor pattern: `anchorKind: "after-heading"` + `anchorHeading: "Request Flow"` (or whatever section header the diagram illustrates) is the right default. Diagram types Mermaid supports well from text descriptions: flowchart for code paths and decision trees, sequenceDiagram for request/response flows between services, stateDiagram-v2 for lifecycle/state machines, classDiagram for domain models, erDiagram for database schemas. A diagram is worth adding when prose alone makes the reader trace through entities and arrows mentally — if you find yourself writing "X calls Y which then writes to Z and notifies W", that paragraph is a diagram in disguise. Don't add charts for decorative reasons (a "system overview" diagram that just boxes-and-arrows the same things the prose already names is noise). Call `wiki_replace_chart` with the original `chartId` (printed in the inserted block's marker comment, e.g., `<!-- chart:auto-flowchart-7e9f1a3 -->`) when iterating; do not delete + reinsert. The `caption` parameter renders as italic `*Figure: ...*` below the chart and is a good place to call out one specific thing the diagram clarifies that the surrounding prose doesn't.

records.text: When extending the review bridge with a new endpoint, three places must be wired together: (1) `src/wiki/review-bridge.ts` — add the route handler inside `createReviewBridgeHandler`, expose the path in the returned handler shape AND in the health response payload, and add a new `ReviewBridgeErrorCode` value if the endpoint can fail in a way distinct from existing codes. (2) `docs/.vitepress/plugins/review-bridge-plugin.ts` — add the path constant, pass it via `createReviewBridgeHandler` options, and add it to the middleware's path-allowlist (`if requestPath !== HEALTH_PATH && requestPath !== EXECUTE_PATH && requestPath !== NEW_PATH`) — forgetting this last step means the embedded same-origin bridge silently 404s for the new endpoint while the standalone token-gated bridge works fine. (3) `test/review-bridge.test.ts` — the existing health-check test does `assert.deepEqual` on the entire response object, so every new field added to the health payload requires updating that assertion in lockstep or the test fails. records.text: When publishing dendrite-wiki-mcp to npm, the `files` field in package.json must be `dist/src` (not `dist`). The wider `dist` value drags in `dist/docs/.vitepress/*` and `dist/scripts/*` because tsconfig.json includes those source directories — but at runtime nothing in the published package needs them: the bin entries are `dist/src/index.js` and `dist/src/cli.js`, no src/* code references compiled scripts/ or docs/.vitepress/ outputs (verified — no `__dirname`/`fileURLToPath`/`import.meta.url` lookups in src/, and install.ts embeds all seeded content as strings rather than copying from the package install location). Tightening to `dist/src` cut tarball from 34 files / 90.3kB to 27 files / 87.3kB. If tsconfig.json's `include` ever shrinks to `["src/**/*.ts"]`, the wider `dist` value would become safe again — but as long as docs/.vitepress and scripts are typechecked through the same tsconfig, keep `dist/src`.

items.summary: Memory has no supporting sources: When using prefers-reduced-motion to disable Vue/CSS animations, do NOT just set `animation: none !important` — that...
items.reason: No supporting sources are attached, so the memory cannot yet be traced back to code, commands, wiki pages, or decisions.

records.text: When using prefers-reduced-motion to disable Vue/CSS animations, do NOT just set `animation: none !important` — that leaves any element whose visible state depends on the animation's `forwards` final-frame stuck at its initial-frame state (e.g. an SVG checkmark with stroke-dashoffset:22 stays invisible because the draw-in animation never runs to set offset:0). Instead, in the reduced-motion media query EXPLICITLY apply the resolved final values (`stroke-dashoffset: 0 !important; opacity: 1 !important; transform: none !important`) so reduced-motion users still see the completed visual, just without the bloom. Verified concretely on MaintenanceReviewBoard.vue's .rb-completion__seal-ring/check and .rb-completion__label after agent-browser eval revealed the seal's computed dashoffset never moved off 100.5px on a system with prefers-reduced-motion: reduce. Pattern applies to any draw-in / fade-in / scale-in animation gated by a media query.

items.summary: Memory has no supporting sources: When walking TypeScript ASTs purely for declaration/JSDoc extraction (no type checking), prefer `ts.createSourceFile(...
items.reason: No supporting sources are attached, so the memory cannot yet be traced back to code, commands, wiki pages, or decisions.

records.text: When walking TypeScript ASTs purely for declaration/JSDoc extraction (no type checking), prefer `ts.createSourceFile(path, text, target, setParentNodes=true, ts.ScriptKind.TS)` over `ts.createProgram(...).getSourceFile()`. Two reasons learned the hard way during the API extractor implementation: (1) Program-loaded SourceFiles do NOT have parent pointers set, so anything that walks parents — `ts.getJSDocCommentsAndTags(node)`, `node.getText()`, `node.getSourceFile()`, the TypeScript printer's printNode — silently returns empty/undefined or throws. (2) JSDoc is still attached to declaration nodes via the `.jsDoc` property regardless of parent pointers, so direct `(node as { jsDoc?: ts.JSDoc[] }).jsDoc` access works without a Program. Take the LAST element of the `.jsDoc` array as the owning doc — file-level JSDoc gets attached to the first declaration too, so the first entry can be a leftover from leading file comments. Use `setParentNodes:true` only when you actually need parent traversal; you almost always do once you start using getText() or printer.

records.text: Skill enforcement decision: agents drift on tool discipline (documented in mem_7d531792). The skill discovery flow can't depend on agents remembering to call wiki_context. The fix is hook-injected enforcement: a UserPromptSubmit hook fires wiki_context automatically on every user prompt and injects matched skill summaries; a PreToolUse hook on Edit/Write fires a quick scope match against the file path/language and injects matching skill summaries as a system reminder. Hooks ship in the installer under .github/hooks/ alongside session-start, session-handoff, and benchmark hooks.

records.text: Skills-as-memory architecture decision: skills are a new memory kind ('skill') that extends the existing memory record with a scope schema (filePatterns, frameworks, languages, taskKeywords, matchMode). The frontier coding agent (Claude/Cursor/etc) — not a local LLM — picks which skills to use via a two-phase fetch: (1) wiki_context returns skill *summaries* matching the task scope; (2) the agent calls wiki_skill_load(id) for the ones it picks. This mirrors the existing wiki_search → wiki_read pattern and avoids requiring a local LLM. Free tier (no gating). Build phases S1–S7 in skills-as-memory.md.

records.text: Apply flow on the review board MUST stay reactive — no full page resets after a click. Two pieces are required: (1) `runActionViaBridge` accepts an `onAccepted` callback that fires the moment the bridge POST returns 200 (BEFORE the 1.4s completion-overlay hold and `refreshBoardData()`). The preview modal's apply handler passes `onAccepted: () => closePreviewModal()` so the modal disappears the instant the action is accepted, letting the per-item "✓ Done" overlay carry the affirmation in place. (2) `reseedExpandedGroupsIfNeeded` MUST seed expand state ONCE on the first non-empty inbox snapshot and never reseed again. The naive signature based on `groups.map(g=>g.key).sort().join('|')` clobbers the operator's manual expand/collapse choices whenever a purpose group becomes empty (and gets filtered out of `groupedWorkItems`), making the page feel like it "reset" after applying the last item in a group. Use a constant signature ('seeded') so reseed is a one-time bootstrap.

Design rule: Stop is now denied if `edits >= MEMORY_REMEMBER_REQUIRED_EDITS` (currently 1) AND `lastMemoryRememberAt` is null, mirroring the existing wiki_log gate. The constant lives in BOTH the dogfood `.claude/hooks/pre-stop-block.mjs` AND the installer template `buildPreStopBlockHook()` in src/install.ts — these two must stay byte-for-byte identical because test/install.test.ts subtest 8 ("installer-inlined ritual hook scripts stay byte-for-byte identical to .claude/hooks/*.mjs") reads the dogfood file and the installer output, then asserts strict equality. WHY this gate matters: drift warnings mem_7d531792 and mem_5480f5cc documented that the agent reliably called wiki_log but silently dropped memory_remember mid-session. Without this gate, every session that edits without depositing a lesson silently loses ground — the product's whole value proposition depends on lessons accumulating, not just on logs accumulating. WHY the threshold is 1 (not 3 like memory_handoff): even a trivial edit may produce a durable lesson worth capturing; the why-linter (B10, planned) will reject causal-less lessons so junk deposits get filtered at write-time rather than via threshold. WHY the SessionStart text was updated in BOTH src/install.ts builder AND .claude/settings.json: the dogfood file is read by Claude Code on session start and the installer writes the same text into newly-installed projects; if either drifts the operator gets inconsistent expectations. Test added: assertions in test/install.test.ts that the rendered pre-stop-block.mjs contains `MEMORY_REMEMBER_REQUIRED_EDITS`, `missing.push('memory_remember')`, and reads `lastMemoryRememberAt`. The byte-for-byte parity test already covers dogfood/installer drift implicitly.

records.text: B3 (operator phrasebook + ritual-hook pattern matcher) shipped 2026-05-11. New module src/wiki/operator-phrasebook.ts exports OPERATOR_PHRASE_RULES (19 entries across 4 categories: durable-intent, scope-setting, session-boundary, reviewer-control), matchOperatorPhrases(prompt), and formatOperatorPhraseNudges(matches). New wiki page docs/wiki/operator-phrasebook.md documents the phrases-as-API contract with tables grouped by category and links to the source-of-truth module. WHY this matters: the agent's memory_remember quality is bounded by how much causal language the operator puts in the prompt; high-signal multi-word triggers like "from now on", "the reason we", "whenever you're editing", "wrapping up", "pin that", "promote that" are unambiguous enough that nudging the agent at UserPromptSubmit time has near-zero false-positive risk and lubricates the right post-action behavior. WHY multi-word patterns: "always" or "never" alone fire constantly on routine prose; phrases like "from now on" are rare enough that a hit reliably means a durable rule. Word-boundary regex applies to the right edge only — patterns starting with a content word (not preceded by other letters in practice) — so "from now once" does NOT match "from now on". MATCH ALGORITHM: lowercase the prompt, scan for each rule's substring, check the next character is non-alphanumeric (or EOS). Returns one match per rule. Multiple categories can fire on a single prompt; each surfaces independently. CLI WIRING: ritual:hook (Claude/Codex UserPromptSubmit) and ritual:cursor-hook (Cursor beforeMCPExecution) both read stdin JSON, extract payload.prompt if present, run matchOperatorPhrases, and append the formatted [DENDRITE OPERATOR PHRASEBOOK] block to the existing [DENDRITE RITUAL CHECKPOINT] additionalContext. When Cursor's payload omits prompt (the typical case), matchOperatorPhrases returns [] silently — no error, no nudge. Critical design decision: the hooks NEVER block — this is purely advisory. Tests at test/operator-phrasebook.test.ts (13 tests covering empty/missing prompt, every category, word-boundary, multi-category, case-insensitivity, casing preservation, routine-prose quiet path, format-output shape, vocabulary). Wired into VitePress sidebar under Wiki Pages section. NOTE on mcp-server.test.ts flakiness observed: 2/25 tests (apply wiki promotion + capture session handoff) occasionally fail with MCP SDK 60s protocol timeouts under load — these are pre-existing infrastructure flakiness, NOT B3 regressions. Re-running the same tests in isolation passes cleanly. B3 doesn't touch src/server.ts at all.

records.text: B4 (working-memory current-goal slot) shipped 2026-05-10. Added `currentGoal: { query: string; setAt: string } | null` to RitualState in src/wiki/ritual-state.ts. Updated recordToolCall(toolName, metadata?) to optionally accept `{ query?: string }` — wiki_context calls now thread their query through wrapToolResponse(toolName, baseText, { query }) in src/server.ts. The current-goal is replaced when the new wiki_context query has Jaccard token-overlap below CURRENT_GOAL_REPLACE_THRESHOLD=0.5 with the existing goal; near-duplicates leave the slot alone. Surfaced in the ritual checkpoint footer above any reminders: `Current goal: "<query>" (set <relative time> ago)`. WHY this matters: the operator should be able to glance at any tool response footer and verify the agent's perceived task matches what they asked for — drift between the asked task and the agent's interpretation becomes visible at every tool call instead of hidden until end-of-session. Footer now also fires when only a goal is set (no reminders), so the goal is always visible after the first wiki_context call. Persists through readPersistedRitualState round-trip. Helpers: tokenizeGoalQuery (lowercase + length>=3 + Set-dedup), jaccardOverlap (returns 0 for disjoint or empty sets, 1 for identical sets), formatRelativeAge (just now / N minutes ago / N hours ago / N days ago). WHY length>=3 not stopword-list: technical task descriptions like "fix the api" share "the" as stopword AND "fix"/"api" as content; length filter is brittle either way but length-3 floor keeps short content tokens like "fix"/"use"/"add"/"old" while dropping 1-2 char noise. Trade-off accepted: "the" is retained in tokens, which slightly inflates similarity for queries that both contain it, but the 0.5 threshold is tolerant enough that distinct tasks still pass it. Tests at test/ritual-state.test.ts added 8 B4-tagged subtests (set, replace, near-dup ignore, Jaccard math, tokenizer, persistence round-trip, footer surfacing, footer-empty-when-nothing, relative-age formatting). All 27 ritual-state tests + 25 MCP server tests + 8 install tests pass.

records.text: B5 (backlog-aware session-start banner) shipped 2026-05-10. Added `summarizeMemoryBacklog()` in src/wiki/memory-store.ts as a LIGHTWEIGHT helper distinct from `reviewProjectMemories` — counts only three buckets (promotionReady, skillPromotionReady, staleUnsupported) without running duplicate/contradiction/near-duplicate detection, because the briefing banner is a session-nudge not a full review. Returns `MemoryBacklogSummary { promotionReady, skillPromotionReady, staleUnsupported, total }`. WHY a separate helper instead of reusing reviewProjectMemories: review runs O(N²)-ish duplicate scans and a graph walk to find contradictions — paying that cost on every wiki_context call would be wasteful when the operator just wants three numbers. WHY include the banner on EVERY wiki_context call (not just first-of-session as the original roadmap acceptance criteria suggested): the cache invalidates on any memory mutation so the banner cannot lie; the operator runs multiple wiki_context calls per session and seeing the backlog at the top of each briefing reinforces metacognition; if counts drop to zero the banner suppresses entirely so it never becomes noise. WHY dual-counting is intentional: a single record can legitimately count in BOTH promotionReady AND skillPromotionReady — these are distinct triage actions (canonicalize to wiki vs scope as skill), so the operator should see both numbers. Matches reviewProjectMemories which emits separate findings for each applicable bucket. Wired into store.ts: WikiContextResult gained a `memoryBacklog: MemoryBacklogSummary` field; buildContextBriefing prepends "Memory backlog: N promotion-ready, M skill-promotion-ready, K stale-unsupported memories waiting in the inbox. Call wiki_maintenance_inbox to triage, or memory_review for the full findings list." when total > 0. Tests at test/memory-backlog.test.ts: empty store, mixed buckets, threshold honoring, briefing emit, briefing suppress on zero — all 5 passing. WHY tests use mkdtemp + DENDRITE_WIKI_DATA_DIR-style root passing: memory store reads from `<root>/local-data/project-memories.json` via resolveProjectMemoryStorePath(root), so isolated per-test dirs prevent cross-test contamination; process.chdir + dynamic import with cache-buster query string for the buildWikiContext path which captures cwd at module load.

records.text: B6 (synaptic-pruning auto-archive) shipped 2026-05-11. New module src/wiki/memory-auto-archive.ts mirrors the existing src/wiki/auto-promote.ts shape: deterministic candidate selection in `findMemoryAutoArchiveCandidates`, env-var-gated apply in `autoArchiveMemories`, per-sweep cap (default 25), dry-run mode. CRITERIA (all must hold for a memory to qualify): status==='active', kind not in {skill, handoff}, recallCount===0, sources.length===0, salience unset or 0, ageInDays >= staleAfterDays (default 30). Archive is reversible via existing memory_restore. WHY salience>0 blocks pruning even if everything else qualifies: operator pinning (or propagation-floor inheritance) is the explicit "keep this" signal — the brain analog is that salience-tagged memories resist forgetting curves. WHY kind!=skill: skills are procedural memory anchored to scope, not subject to recall-count pruning; they age out via wiki page promotion instead. WHY kind!=handoff: handoffs are short-lived continuation notes that age out naturally as the next session reads them; auto-archiving them would create churn against memory_handoff's design. Surfaces: (1) CLI `dendrite-wiki memory:auto-archive [--dry-run]` with same UX as memory:auto-promote — env var required for apply, dry-run always available; (2) MCP tool `memory_auto_archive` with optional {dryRun, maxPerSweep, staleAfterDays} — gated via GATED_TOOL_NAMES so it can't run before wiki_context. WHY both surfaces: CLI for nightly cron / git hook integration, MCP tool for chat-driven triage ("clean the review board" phrasebook trigger). MCP tool was added to mcp-server.test.ts tool list (alphabetically first among memory_* tools) and ritual-state.test.ts gated list. Tests at test/memory-auto-archive.test.ts: 9 tests covering each predicate field individually blocking archive, dry-run quiet path, env-var-gated refuse, apply success, maxPerSweep cap, reversibility round-trip via memory_restore, env-var matching (on/true/1/yes/enable/enabled), custom staleAfterDays threshold. records.text: B9 (sleep-cycle consolidation pass) shipped 2026-05-11. New module src/wiki/consolidate.ts orchestrates the three input streams (memory_review findings + auto-promote candidates + auto-archive candidates) into a single clustered report via union-find on shared anchors. ANCHOR DEFINITION: each finding's anchors are the union of its source record's relatedFiles + `page:<slug>` for each relatedPages entry + `tag:<name>` for each tag, all lowercased. Two findings end up in the same cluster when their anchor sets share at least one element (transitive via union-find — A shares with B, B shares with C → A/B/C all in one cluster). Findings with no anchors go to `orphans` so they aren't lost. Clusters sorted by descending size then lexicographic id for stability. WHY clustering by anchors not by kind: the operator's natural triage unit is "everything about this file/page" not "everything stale", so a single click should let them address all findings touching one area. CLI: `dendrite-wiki consolidate [--apply] [--max-clusters N]` — default dry-run prints the report; --apply orchestrates auto-promote + auto-archive sweeps under shared cap (default 10 split half-and-half between the two). THREE env vars required for full --apply: DENDRITE_AUTO_CONSOLIDATE=on (gate the orchestration), DENDRITE_AUTO_PROMOTE=on (for promote sub-sweep), DENDRITE_AUTO_ARCHIVE=on (for archive sub-sweep). WHY three gates not one: each sub-sweep already has its own opt-in semantics; collapsing them into one consolidate gate would surprise operators who set DENDRITE_AUTO_CONSOLIDATE expecting just clustering. Tests at test/consolidate.test.ts: 9 tests covering empty store, single-cluster, multi-cluster, orphans, maxClusters cap, descending-size sort + id tiebreak, env-var-gated apply refuse, toConsolidateFindings anchor extraction (file/page-prefixed/tag-prefixed), isAutoConsolidateEnabled env-var matching. NO MCP TOOL surface yet (CLI-only). The future surface can be `wiki_consolidate` if real usage shows chat-driven consolidation requests are common; until then the operator runs the CLI from a terminal or wires it into a cron / git hook. All 171/171 tests across 18 affected test files green: operator-phrasebook (13) + memory-auto-archive (9) + consolidate (9) + memory-backlog (5) + memory-salience (9) + memory-why-linter (7) + ritual-state (27) + install (8) + wiki-store (11) + skill-promotion (10) + skill-portability (5) + memory-skill-kind (7) + diff-context (5) + embedding-provider (8) + review-bridge (7) + memory-ranking + skill-load-and-context (13) + context-cache (3) + others.

records.text:
Before committing changes that land ahead of an `npm publish` workflow on this repo, run `npm run check` locally end-to-end (wiki:refresh → build → tests → docs:build) — not just `npx tsx --test test/*.test.ts`. The full check is the same script CI runs pre-publish, and the focused test sweep can miss flakes that only surface under different timing.

Why: in 0.4.0-alpha.1, two synthesis tests in mcp-server.test.ts (bounded-proposal-synthesis at line 1156, agent-handoff-synthesis at line 1193) read problemFixtureRoot directly while two preceding apply tests mutate that same fixture in-place and rely on try/finally to restore. On a slow CI runner the spawned MCP server raced the finally restore, so the synthesis tests saw a mid-edit fixture (AGENTS.md temporarily short → 1 proposal instead of 2). Local Windows machine never reproduced; CI did. The user had to run the publish workflow once to find it. Pattern: tests that share a mutable fixture with apply tests are flaky in proportion to runner slowness — fix is to mkdtemp+fs.cp the fixture into a per-test temp dir, same pattern the memory tests already use.

How to apply: any time a commit chain is about to be pushed to a branch that triggers an `npm publish` or release workflow, run `npm run check` locally first. If `check` is green and only then commit + push. If a test in mcp-server.test.ts uses a shared mutable fixture (problemFixtureRoot or fixtureRoot) without an mkdtemp wrapper, treat it as a candidate for the isolation pattern even if it passes locally.

records.text: Browser print preview is theme-independent: a `@media print` block in CSS fires when ANY user triggers print (Ctrl+P or window.print()) regardless of which retro theme they have active. So put print rules at the global level, NOT scoped to `html[data-dendrite-theme='X']` like screen-themed retro overrides. The screen-themed @media print scoping (e.g. `html[data-dendrite-theme='selectric'] .VPNav`) only fires when the operator was already in that theme — defeats the goal of "one-click pretty print from any theme." Use bare selectors inside `@media print { ... }`. Also: `window.print()` cannot bypass the browser's print dialog (no browser allows programmatic-print without user gesture), so the UX goal is to make the *preview that appears in that dialog* look right by default — don't try to skip the dialog. Set `@page { margin: 0.7in 0.6in 0.8in 0.6in }` for sensible margins that play well with default browser headers/footers. Verification limitation: agent-browser CLI's evaluate doesn't expose Playwright's `page.emulateMedia({media:'print'})` — to visually verify, the operator has to hit Ctrl+P in a real browser, OR you can launch Chrome with `--emulate-media=print` flag.

records.text: CodeMirror 6 inherits VitePress theme palette automatically when the editor is configured with `EditorView.theme({ '&': { color: 'var(--vp-c-text-1)', backgroundColor: 'transparent', fontFamily: 'var(--vp-font-family-mono, monospace)' }, ... })`. Pass `{ dark: false }` as the second argument so CM doesn't add its own `cm-theme-dark` class that would fight the parent CSS — the dark/light decision lives in the VitePress theme tokens, not in CodeMirror. Using `backgroundColor: 'transparent'` lets the modal's parent `var(--vp-c-bg)` show through, which means switching the dendrite retro theme (Amber/WordPerfect/Selectric) live re-styles the editor without touching CM internals. Required deps for a minimal markdown editor: `codemirror` (umbrella + basicSetup), `@codemirror/state`, `@codemirror/view`, `@codemirror/lang-markdown`, plus `@codemirror/commands` (defaultKeymap, history, historyKeymap), `@codemirror/language` (syntaxHighlighting, defaultHighlightStyle, bracketMatching, indentOnInput), `@codemirror/search` (searchKeymap). For read-only mode use BOTH `EditorState.readOnly.of(true)` AND `EditorView.editable.of(false)` — the first blocks programmatic transactions, the second blocks DOM contentEditable.

records.text: Cursor's beforeMCPExecution hook expects a different output JSON shape than Claude Code/Codex hooks: `{permission: 'allow'|'deny'|'ask', userMessage?, agentMessage?}` instead of `{hookSpecificOutput: {additionalContext: ...}}`. The agentMessage field is what gets injected into the agent's context. Stay quiet (empty stdout) when no reminders apply, output `{permission: 'allow', agentMessage: ...}` when ritual gaps need surfacing. Never use deny unless you mean to block — it's heavy-handed for a documentation tool. Implementation: separate ritual:cursor-hook subcommand in cli.ts shares the same readPersistedRitualState() + computeRemindersForState() pipeline as ritual:hook but emits the Cursor-shaped payload. install.ts writes .cursor/hooks.json with `{version:1, hooks:{beforeMCPExecution:[{command:"npx -y dendrite-wiki ritual:cursor-hook"}]}}` (no inner `hooks: [...]` wrapper unlike Claude Code's nested structure). This is the right hook event because Cursor's beforeSubmitPrompt cannot inject prompts (observe-only), so beforeMCPExecution — which fires before every MCP call across all servers — is the only enforcement point.

records.text: Durable lesson from the C1-C7 implementation campaign: the maintenance-inbox snapshot is the load-bearing integration point for any feature that wants operator review. New action types only become user-visible when they're (a) added to MaintenanceInboxActionHint kind/tool unions, (b) attached to a snapshot section's items[].actions array, (c) routed by findMaintenanceInboxAction's source-type chain, AND (d) handled by executeMaintenanceAction's switch statement. Skipping any one of those four wires the action into the test fixtures without making it executable. The pattern: snapshot-side wiring + action-runner switch case + source-type addition to ResolvedMaintenanceInboxAction['source']. When adding a new finding type (memory review, observation cluster), update both buildMaintenanceInboxSnapshot (data shape) AND findMaintenanceInboxAction (resolver) — the existing memory-bucket code is the canonical template to copy.

records.text: Even with a strict SessionStart hook in .claude/settings.json that explicitly instructs the agent to call wiki_context and capture lessons via memory_remember every session, the agent drifts during long implementation sessions. Across 12+ implementation passes in one Claude Code session, the agent called wiki_log routinely (good — keeps project log current) but stopped calling memory_remember almost entirely, missing real durable lessons (the bridge memoryFindings bug, the supersede pattern, the logic-divergence pitfall, the SSE+fs.watch design, etc.). The hook only fires at session START; it doesn't re-prompt as the session goes on. Real fix candidates: (a) periodic mid-session reminders if the agent harness supports them; (b) bake memory_remember into the post-pass ritual so it's as habitual as wiki_log + npm run check + git commit; (c) add a 'memory hygiene' lint that flags long sessions with high commit count but zero new memories. The deeper signal: hooks are necessary but not sufficient for routine agent discipline; the workflow has to make the right action the easy action.

MemoryStorage interface has 11 methods now: readMemoryStore/writeMemoryStore, readMemoryEdges/writeMemoryEdges, appendObservationLine/readObservationLines/writeObservationLines, readRitualState/writeRitualState, readPageDriftSnoozes/writePageDriftSnoozes. FilesystemMemoryStorage absorbs all writes; brain modules import zero `fs` references on the canonical-state path. 554/554 tests green. Phase 2 starts on memory-promotion.ts.

Next steps:
- After Phase 2: Phase 3 vocabulary rename (WikiClaimSourceKind → MemorySourceKind, relatedPages → relatedDocuments, librarian audit categories split). Should be the smallest phase — mostly mechanical renames with deprecation aliases. Then Phase 4 monorepo split (npm workspaces), Phase 5 publish & second-project dogfood.

Recommend merging now — Phase 1 is a coherent unit and squashing 5 commits into one main commit loses the per-slice design narrative; granular history is better for the eventual @rarusoft/dendrite-memory CHANGELOG.md.
- Phase 2 starts: CanonicalTarget abstraction. memory-promotion.ts is the analog of memory-store.ts for that phase — currently calls writeWikiPage / readWikiPage / appendProjectLog / pagePathFromSlug directly from src/wiki/store.ts. Same shape: define a CanonicalTarget interface (applyPromotion / readExistingContent / appendChangeLog methods), implement WikiCanonicalTarget that wraps the current wiki-page writes, refactor memory-promotion.ts to take a CanonicalTarget instead of calling store.ts directly. auto-promote.ts and consolidate.ts inherit the leak transitively and clean up automatically once memory-promotion uses the interface.
- Worth a benchmark snapshot run before merging Phase 1 to confirm the async cascade through wrapToolResponse has no measurable per-tool-call latency cost. Filesystem write is microseconds at dogfood scale, should be invisible, but worth confirming with the benchmark log entries.

Open questions:
- MCP server name and tool-name compatibility post-extraction: keep `dendrite-wiki-mcp` as the umbrella binary name (so existing operator configs don't break) while introducing @rarusoft/dendrite-memory as the underlying brain dependency. Should this be locked in writing in product-vision.md before Phase 4 starts, or decided per-PR?

Same pattern as MemoryStorage. Worth pinning this as an explicit decision in the roadmap before Phase 2 starts to avoid the constructor-shape pivot that happened mid-Phase-1 between slices 1 and 2.
- Should the contract assertion's allowlist grow as Phase 2+ migrates more modules, or should each phase define its own contract test? Recommend: each phase adds a separate test file with its scoped contract — the per-phase tests serve as living documentation of what each phase delivered, and a single growing assertion would lose that narrative.
- When should the no-direct-fs contract list expand? Argument for expanding now: every brain module that writes JSON should go through the adapter for uniformity. Argument against: embedding cache, benchmark snapshots, skill exports each have very different persistence semantics (some are cache-recreatable, some are operator-shareable, some are migration-able). YAGNI: expand only when a real consumer needs an alternate backend for one of them.

Handoff summary: Phase 1 of the Library Extraction Roadmap started on branch feat/library-extraction (2026-05-12). First slice committed as 134819f: introduced src/wiki/memory-storage.ts with the `MemoryStorage` interface (readMemoryStore + writeMemoryStore) and `FilesystemMemoryStorage` default backend. memory-store.ts refactored to delegate filesystem access to the adapter while keeping its public API byte-identical — every existing entry point keeps the `root: string = process.cwd()` parameter and async signature. `resolveProjectMemoryStorePath` is now a legacy alias that calls into the new single-source-of-truth `resolveMemoryStorePath` in memory-storage.ts. `ProjectMemoryStoreFile` was promoted from internal interface to public export so the adapter can reference the schema; type-only import in memory-storage.ts keeps the dependency graph acyclic at runtime. 6 new adapter tests pin the contract: round-trip, missing file, whitespace file, mkdir-on-write, DENDRITE_WIKI_DATA_DIR env override, direct-path constructor. Total 539/539 tests green. Branch is one commit ahead of main; main has 16 commits ahead of upstream from the prior multi-session arc (per-page badges + librarian + payload trim + Phase 0 audit). Phase 1 remaining slices: memory-edges.ts (memory-trails JSONs under local-data/memory-edges/), raw-observations.ts (raw-observations.jsonl + per-session classifier output), ritual-state.ts (ritual-state.json), page-drift-snoozes.ts (page-drift-snoozes.json). Each is its own focused PR migrating one module's read/write to the adapter while keeping its public API stable. Phase 1 finishes when every persistent file the brain touches goes through MemoryStorage.

Next steps:
- After memory-edges.ts: raw-observations.ts. It's an append-only JSONL stream — the adapter likely needs an `appendObservation` method that's cheaper than read-whole-file-write-whole-file. Decide whether to keep the coarse-grained pattern (write the whole file every append, fine for the 5000-line cap) or add a targeted append method.
- Migrate src/wiki/memory-edges.ts to the MemoryStorage adapter. The edges file structure under local-data/memory-edges/ probably needs the adapter interface to grow new methods (readEdges, writeEdges) — keep the additions minimal and coarse-grained (read-whole-file / write-whole-file, not row-level) so future SQLite backends don't have to model transactional semantics yet.
- Then page-drift-snoozes.ts. Wiki-side concern but currently lives under local-data/, so it also needs the adapter. Could route through a wiki-side storage adapter or share the brain's adapter; defer the design call until that file is being migrated.
- Then ritual-state.ts. Single small JSON file — should be a quick migration mirroring the memory-store pattern.
- When Phase 1 wraps, decide before Phase 2 starts: does CanonicalTarget belong in `@rarusoft/dendrite-memory` (as an interface) or `@rarusoft/dendrite-wiki` (as the implementation only)? The interface needs to live in memory because memory-promotion calls it; the wiki implementation lives in wiki. So interface in brain, implementation in wiki adapter — same pattern as MemoryStorage.

Open questions:
- raw-observations.jsonl is appended-to on every agent tool call (the observations:capture hook). The current pattern is line-cap on every write. If we route through a coarse-grained read-whole / write-whole adapter, we're rewriting the whole file on every tool call — currently fine because the cap is 5000 lines but worth measuring before Phase 4. Maybe a targeted appendLine method on MemoryStorage?
- Should MemoryStorage grow new method names per persistent file (readMemoryStore, readEdges, readRitualState, readObservations) or be generic on a key (`read(key)`, `write(key, value)`)? Named methods are more discoverable and let TypeScript narrow the return type; generic key/value is more flexible but loses type safety. Recommendation: named methods, accept the longer interface — readability beats elegance.
- Should there be a module-level singleton MemoryStorage instance with a setDefaultMemoryStorage() setter, ahead of Phase 4's monorepo split? Argument for: simpler call sites once a second backend exists. Argument against: premature abstraction — no second backend yet, and the per-call `createFilesystemMemoryStorage(root)` pattern is cheap. Defer until a real consumer asks.
- When memory-edges.ts migrates, do its read/write helpers need to be exported the way memory-store's resolveProjectMemoryStorePath is, or are they all internal to the module? Quick grep before migrating will tell us how much backward-compat work is involved.

Handoff summary: Phase 1 slice 2 of the Library Extraction Roadmap landed on feat/library-extraction (commit a409baf, 2026-05-12). Migrated src/wiki/memory-edges.ts to the MemoryStorage adapter. Constructor reshape: FilesystemMemoryStorage now takes a dataDir (was a store-file path in slice 1) so every sibling persistent file shares one configured location and exposes `memoryStorePath` / `memoryEdgesPath` getters. Interface grew readMemoryEdges/writeMemoryEdges. The atomic tmp+rename write strategy + per-path in-process write queue moved INTO the adapter as implementation details — brain code no longer touches `fs` for edges. ProjectMemoryEdgesFile promoted to public export. New resolveMemoryDataDir(root) + resolveMemoryEdgesPath(root) helpers mirror the slice-1 resolveMemoryStorePath. 4 new tests added (round-trip, empty default, concurrent-writes-serialized-no-tmp-stragglers, env-var override). Slice 1's direct-constructor test rewritten to use the new dataDir shape. 543/543 tests green (slice 1 had 539). Branch is 2 commits ahead of main now (134819f + a409baf). Phase 1 remaining: raw-observations.ts (append-only JSONL stream — needs a design call), ritual-state.ts (single small JSON), page-drift-snoozes.ts (wiki-side but lives under local-data/).

Next steps:
- Slice 3: raw-observations.ts. The trickier one because the file is appended on every agent tool call via the observations:capture hook with a 5000-line cap. Design call: (a) keep coarse-grained read-whole + write-whole adapter methods and accept the per-call file rewrite cost (it's working today), or (b) add a targeted appendObservation method to the adapter interface and have the filesystem implementation use fs.appendFile + a periodic cap pass. Recommendation: (a) for now — simpler, no perf measurement says it's a problem at the dogfood scale. Revisit if a second-project dogfood surfaces friction.
- Slice 4: ritual-state.ts. Should be the quickest of the remaining migrations — single small JSON, same shape as memory-store but smaller. Mirror the slice-1 pattern.
- Slice 5: page-drift-snoozes.ts. Wiki-side concern but lives under local-data/. Two options: (a) route through the same MemoryStorage adapter even though it's not brain state — simpler, accepts that the adapter name is now slightly inaccurate; or (b) introduce a sibling WikiStorage adapter for wiki-side persistent files. Recommendation: (a) for Phase 1 — the goal is to remove direct `fs` imports from brain-adjacent modules. Phase 4's monorepo split will be the natural moment to introduce WikiStorage as a separate adapter living in @rarusoft/dendrite-wiki.
- When Phase 1 wraps (all five modules migrated): no module under src/wiki/ should import from `node:fs` directly except inside `memory-storage.ts`. Add a lint rule or a test assertion to lock that property in place before Phase 2 starts.

Open questions:
- page-drift-snoozes is small + low-volume. Doesn't need atomicity guarantees as strong as edges. Should slice 5 route it through MemoryStorage's writeMemoryStore-style coarse method, or introduce a generic `read(key)` / `write(key, value)` to the adapter for one-off small files? Generic keyed access would be useful for any future small persistent state.
- raw-observations.jsonl is appended on every tool call. Coarse-grained read-whole/write-whole is fine at 5000 lines but if it grows to 50k it becomes a real cost. Should slice 3 add appendObservation now as a forward-looking API, or wait until the perf bites?
- ritual-state writes happen on every tool call too (the ritual checkpoint footer). Currently src/wiki/ritual-state.ts has no `fs` imports — let me verify before slice 4. If it doesn't persist anything, slice 4 may collapse to a no-op.
- When Phase 1 wraps, should we add a static check (lint rule or test) that no module under src/wiki/ imports from `node:fs` except memory-storage.ts? Would lock the no-direct-fs property in place against future regressions.

Handoff summary: Phase 1 slice 3 of the Library Extraction Roadmap landed on feat/library-extraction (commit b0eca34, 2026-05-12). Migrated src/wiki/raw-observations.ts to the MemoryStorage adapter. Made the targeted-append design call (vs coarse read-whole/write-whole) because observations:capture fires on every agent tool call and read-whole/write-whole on a 5000-line cap would have been ~50x the I/O. Interface grew three methods: appendObservationLine (O(1) via fs.appendFile, naturally atomic for sub-PIPE_BUF payloads via POSIX O_APPEND), readObservationLines (returns raw non-empty string lines, brain owns JSON parse), writeObservationLines (coarse rewrite used only by lazy retention). raw-observations.ts now imports nothing from `fs` or `path` directly. resolveRawObservationsPath is a legacy alias to the new single-source-of-truth in memory-storage.ts. 3 new adapter tests including the append-trim-append compose pattern that catches the missing-trailing-newline regression. 546/546 tests green. Branch is now 3 commits ahead of main (134819f memory-store, a409baf memory-edges, b0eca34 raw-observations). Phase 1 remaining: ritual-state.ts (uses SYNC fs — async-everywhere policy means this is slightly bigger than the other slices, may need to evaluate whether sync was deliberate for hook-path latency) and page-drift-snoozes.ts (straightforward async fs migration, but wiki-side concern living under local-data/ — decide whether to route through MemoryStorage or introduce a sibling WikiStorage adapter for it).

Next steps:
- Slice 4: ritual-state.ts. CAUTION — it uses SYNC fs (mkdirSync, writeFileSync, readFileSync, existsSync). Two options: (a) flip to async on the adapter boundary, accepting that every ritual-state caller becomes async too; (b) keep ritual-state sync and add a parallel sync adapter surface to MemoryStorage. Recommendation: (a) — async-everywhere is the Phase 1 policy and ritual-state writes don't happen on a hot enough path to need sync semantics (the PreEdit/Stop hooks are already process boundaries that can wait for an async write). Verify by grep that the writeRitualState callers are already in async functions before flipping.
- Slice 5: page-drift-snoozes.ts. Wiki-side concern but lives under local-data/. Phase 1's goal is to remove direct `fs` imports from brain-adjacent modules, so the simplest answer is to route through MemoryStorage for now and split into a sibling WikiStorage adapter in Phase 4's monorepo split. Add readPageDriftSnoozes / writePageDriftSnoozes to the interface, mirror the memory-store slice 1 pattern.
- Then merge feat/library-extraction to main before starting Phase 2 (CanonicalTarget abstraction). Phase 2 has a similar shape but for the wiki-write side — memory-promotion.ts is the analog of memory-store.ts for that phase.
- When Phase 1 wraps (slice 4 + slice 5 land): add a test or lint rule that asserts no module under src/wiki/ imports from `node:fs` directly except memory-storage.ts. Locks the no-direct-fs property against future regressions and signals Phase 1 completion.

Open questions:
- Phase 1 has shipped three slices in three commits each as ~250-300 lines of diff. Worth a single squash-merge to main, or keep the granular history? Granular history is more informative for the eventual @rarusoft/dendrite-memory CHANGELOG.md when extracting; squash is cleaner for `git log main`. Recommend granular.
- ritual-state.ts being sync — was that a deliberate choice for hook-path latency or just historical inertia? grep where its read/write fires to check whether the call sites are inside async functions already. If they are, flipping to async is mechanically simple; if some caller is in a sync hook context, may need a sync escape hatch.
- Should the MemoryStorage interface also grow a `flushAll(): Promise<void>` method for future SQLite/HTTP backends that batch writes? Filesystem implementation could no-op. Premature today; revisit when a second backend exists.
- Should we expose a way to swap the default adapter at runtime (setDefaultMemoryStorage), or keep per-call adapter construction as the only path until Phase 4? Per-call is currently cheap (just an object with a string path). A singleton would mean test fixtures could no longer use different roots in the same process, which the current test pattern relies on. Keep per-call.

items.summary: Memory is incubating: Mermaid's Node-mode parser is unreliable for server-side validation.
items.reason: No problems detected. Recalled 0 times, never recalled; 4 days old. Surfaced here so manual archive remains one click away.

records.text: Mermaid's Node-mode parser is unreliable for server-side validation. `mermaid.parse(source, { suppressErrors: true })` returns `false` for almost any non-trivial chart when there's no DOM, because Mermaid internally calls `DOMPurify.addHook` which fails without a browser environment. Charts that render perfectly in the browser get rejected by the parser. So if you need to validate Mermaid source server-side, DON'T just call mermaid.parse(); either install jsdom and polyfill `global.window`/`global.document` BEFORE importing mermaid (heavy but accurate), or use a heuristic validator that catches the actual LLM failure modes (keyword-on-first-line check, bracket balance for truncation detection, connection-operator presence for diagram types that need them — flowchart/sequence/state/class/er, with gantt/pie/journey/timeline exempt — and HTML script/iframe rejection for defense-in-depth on top of mermaid's `securityLevel: 'strict'`). The heuristic approach false-negatives some malformed input but never false-positives valid content; the rendering step in the browser catches the rest at display time, where errors are visible and recoverable.

items.summary: Memory is incubating: Modal responsive layouts at narrow widths: nested flex INSIDE a fixed-viewport grid is great for "always-visible" sti...
items.reason: No problems detected. Recalled 0 times, never recalled; 4 days old. Surfaced here so manual archive remains one click away.

records.text: Modal responsive layouts at narrow widths: nested flex INSIDE a fixed-viewport grid is great for "always-visible" sticky header/footer + scroll-only-the-middle pinning, but it's HOSTILE to content that varies in size at narrow widths. The failure mode: each grid row gets its allocated height, the card inside it has flex children (header, pills, summary, body), and the rigid non-flex children eat all the height before `flex: 1; min-height: 0` on the body can claim its share — body collapses to ~30px with a useless scrollbar. The fix: flip the pattern when the form factor changes. At narrow widths, switch the modal-panel itself to `height: auto; overflow-y: auto`, and set every internal scroll region (cards, text bodies, grids) to `flex: none; overflow: visible` so content flows naturally. Cap individual elements (e.g., `max-height: 18rem` on a code block) so one outlier doesn't dominate, but otherwise let the modal scroll like a mobile sheet. Trigger via container queries (preferred — `@container <name> (max-width: 760px)`) with a `@media (max-width: 900px)` fallback for browsers that don't support container queries (Chrome <105, Firefox <110, Safari <16). Don't try to keep a fixed-viewport modal at narrow widths — the math doesn't work and content gets clipped.

items.summary: Memory is incubating: Module-level default-target DI is the right shape for extracting a wiki-coupled function into a library when the goal...
items.reason: No problems detected. Recalled 0 times, never recalled; 1 day old. Surfaced here so manual archive remains one click away.

records.text: Module-level default-target DI is the right shape for extracting a wiki-coupled function into a library when the goal is to keep callers byte-identical. The brain's CanonicalTarget interface lives next to a tiny registry (setDefaultCanonicalTarget(t) + getDefaultCanonicalTarget() that throws if unset + clearDefaultCanonicalTarget() for tests + hasDefaultCanonicalTarget() for diagnostics). Concrete implementations register themselves at module load via a top-level `setDefaultCanonicalTarget(createImpl())` side effect. Brain functions resolve via getDefaultCanonicalTarget() so their signatures don't change. Consumers that may exercise brain functions before importing any module that triggers registration add a tiny `import './adapter.js'` side-effect at the top of consuming modules (NOT at the top of test files that chdir into a temp fixture in before() — those rely on the test module's dynamic import in before() to load the consumer module post-chdir, so adding the side-effect import at file-top breaks the wikiRoot-capture order). The "every wiki-tier consumer self-registers" pattern is preferable to "central bootstrap" because each consuming module declares its dependency explicitly. This was chosen over passing `target: CanonicalTarget` as a parameter to every brain function because the parameter shape would force every existing call site to change, blocking the slice on a much larger refactor for no real DI advantage at this stage (a single global wiki target serves every brain function in practice).

items.reason: No problems detected. Recalled 1 time, last recalled 0 days ago; 1 day old. Surfaced here so manual archive remains one click away.

records.text: Slice F's per-workspace `"build": "tsc -p tsconfig.json"` script proves the package boundary at the build-system level in addition to npm-test-time contract enforcement. The brain-no-wiki-coupling test asserts no wiki imports at module-text scan time; the standalone build asserts the same at the type-resolution level by refusing to compile if any back-reference exists. They are complementary: the contract test catches the regression immediately on `npm test` (fast, cheap, runs in dev loops); the standalone build catches it on `npm run build -w @rarusoft/dendrite-memory` (the same check the eventual publish flow runs). Both are needed. The contract test alone is insufficient because it only inspects import statement text — it would miss a deep TypeScript type cycle that compiled successfully in the umbrella tsconfig but breaks in the per-workspace one. The standalone build alone is insufficient because tsc errors are slow feedback compared to a focused contract test. Same logic for `wiki-no-brain-internals` + `npm run build -w @rarusoft/dendrite-wiki` — the wiki workspace must declare @rarusoft/dendrite-memory in its package.json dependencies (workspace:* or "*") so the workspace symlink resolution survives an eventual `npm publish` to a registry. Without the explicit dependency, npm publish strips the symlink and the wiki package becomes unresolvable for any downstream consumer.

records.text: store.ts captures `process.cwd()` at module-load time (line 512: `const repoRoot = path.resolve(process.cwd());`), which means tests that need fixture-isolated execution must use ONE temp directory for all phases of work — not one per test. The existing benchmark.test.ts pattern works because it has exactly one test() block; if you copy that pattern with multiple test() blocks each creating their own mkdtemp + chdir + fs.rm cycle, the second test will fail with ENOENT inside listWikiPages because store.ts still holds the path of the deleted first temp dir. The fix: combine multi-phase fixture-dependent tests into a single test() block with phases inside it (see test/report-export.test.ts for the working pattern). The longer-term fix would be to refactor store.ts to lazy-resolve cwd per call, but that touches many call sites and changes module behavior.

records.text: VitePress markdown files are processed as Vue templates, so any literal `</tag>` text inside a markdown file will fail Vue's HTML parser with "Invalid end tag" errors. This bit project-log.md when wiki_log entries from earlier in this session leaked tool-call closing syntax (literal `</entry>` and `</invoke>` strings) into the markdown body. The build error was opaque — `[vite:vue] docs/wiki/project-log.md (169:1408): Invalid end tag` with no clue that the cause was tool-call leakage. Defensive habits: (1) when calling wiki_log, only pass plain prose — never include tool-call XML or anything that looks like an HTML close tag; (2) when npm run docs:build fails with "Invalid end tag" on a wiki page, search the page for any `</word>` patterns that aren't real HTML; (3) consider adding a wiki_log validator that strips or refuses input containing `</[a-zA-Z]+>` patterns to prevent recurrence.
