records.text: When a brain module is coupled to a specific output backend across multiple dimensions (storage I/O + output formatting + target-id resolution + diagnostic display), the cleanest abstraction is a single combined adapter interface, not a split storage-vs-formatter pair. Reason: a destination's storage and format are inherently coupled (Notion's storage isn't filesystem AND its format isn't markdown; same flip applies to every alternative target), so forcing callers to pick two adapters and keep them in sync creates work without correctness benefit. The combined CanonicalTarget interface from Phase 2 has 10 methods spanning four concern groups: (1) Storage — readContent / writeContent / appendChangeLog; (2) Display — formatTargetPath / resolveTitle; (3) Target-id resolution — resolveTargetId (with optional caller-supplied override); (4) Format-specific — resolveSectionHeading / formatPromotionBlock / composeNewContent / isPromotionAlreadyApplied / anchorForHeading. Pattern that works: the brain calls `createWikiCanonicalTarget()` per call (cheap object construction, mirrors Phase 1's per-call MemoryStorage pattern), holds the returned interface as a typed const, and routes every backend interaction through interface methods. Legacy module-level helper functions (e.g., resolvePromotionTargetSlug) are kept as thin pass-throughs to the new module so downstream callers don't need import-path updates during the slice. The test-suite truth-test for an indirection-without-API-change refactor: the full suite must stay green at the same count, because the brain's PUBLIC API is unchanged and the WIKI's PUBLIC API is unchanged — only the internal seam shifted.

records.text: When a polling Vue/d3-force visualization re-runs the simulation on every snapshot update, the fix is a topology fingerprint short-circuit not a debounce: hash the sorted-node-ids + sorted-edges + layout-mode into a string, store it in a let binding outside the rebuild function, and on every snapshot update compute the fresh fingerprint and compare. When unchanged, Object.assign the fresh attribute bag onto the EXISTING simulation node instances in place (positions x/y/vx/vy untouched), bump a reactive tick counter so the SVG re-renders, and return WITHOUT calling forceSimulation again. The simulation keeps running with its current alpha schedule, the visual stays stable, and operators can poll on a 5s cadence without the graph re-laying out under them. When the fingerprint genuinely changes (filter toggle, new node arrived, clustering mode flipped), fall through to a full rebuild path that still preserves prior x/y/vx/vy by id so only newly-appeared nodes seed from scratch. The reason this works: d3-force mutates node objects in place by reference, so updating the SAME object references with new memory.recallCount/updatedAt values without rebuilding the array means d3 picks up the new attribute reads on the next tick without re-initializing forces. Pattern applies to any Vue + d3 force-directed graph that polls live data.

records.text: When a polling-based dev-server visualization causes visible stutter even after a topology-fingerprint stable-poll fix, the real answer is SSE push not faster polling, and the right way to wire SSE in a multi-process project (e.g., MCP server + VitePress dev server share local-data/ JSON files but run as separate Node processes) is to watch the FILESYSTEM not an in-process callback registry. In-process callbacks (like the brain's onMemoryMutation) only fire for mutations that happen inside the same Node process; cross-process mutations are invisible to them. File-watching catches every mutation regardless of which process wrote it, with the cost of debouncing FSWatcher fileName-equality filters down to just the brain-state files the visualization actually depends on (project-memories.json, supervision-changes.jsonl, etc.) so unrelated artifacts don't trigger spurious broadcasts. The SSE payload should be signal-only: emit `event: name\ndata: {"ts":"..."}` and let the client refetch the snapshot on each event. Don't serialize the whole graph into the SSE message — same-origin network is cheap, and signal-only keeps the server stateless about diffs. Keep polling as a safety-net fallback at a slow cadence (60s+) so dropped SSE doesn't freeze the view; the existing stable-poll path makes the fallback ticks visually free when topology is unchanged. Browser EventSource auto-retries on disconnect, so error handling is minimal.

records.text: When a VitePress theme component holds a composable result that contains nested Refs (like UsePageInboxResult { snapshot: Ref<...>, loading: Ref<...> }) AND the component needs to reassign the whole result when an input changes (slug changes), use shallowRef NOT ref. The reason ref() fails here is that ref() auto-unwraps any nested Refs inside its value — so `inbox.value.snapshot` becomes the unwrapped value (not the ref), and the subsequent `.value` access crashes with "Cannot read properties of null (reading 'value')" because the initial null state has no .value property. Vue surfaces this as a generic "Unhandled error during execution of render function at <ComponentName>" warning with no further detail, which makes it surprisingly hard to diagnose. shallowRef preserves the nested refs as-is because the wrapping ref does not recursively unwrap, so `inbox.value.snapshot.value` works correctly at every state. Trade-off: ref()'s auto-unwrap is intended for the common case of "I want to access nested refs without writing .value", but it silently breaks the "I want to hold and pass refs around as a composable's return value" pattern.

records.text: When a wiki adapter package needs to register itself as a brain dependency-injection default at module load, ordering the barrel's re-exports matters because TypeScript ES modules evaluate `export * from './X.js'` lines in source order at the consumer's first import. Putting the side-effect-emitting module FIRST in the barrel (e.g., `export * from './canonical-target.js'` ahead of every other export) guarantees its top-level `setDefaultCanonicalTarget()` call fires before any later import statement runs — so a single `import { anything } from '@rarusoft/dendrite-wiki'` is enough to wire up the brain DI default. This eliminates the need for belt-and-suspenders `import './X/canonical-target.js'` side-effect imports at every entry point because every entry that imports anything from the wiki barrel auto-registers. The only places that still need an explicit `import '@rarusoft/dendrite-wiki';` side-effect line are test files that exercise brain promotion functions WITHOUT importing anything else from the wiki package — they need the registration to fire but don't naturally pull it in. The pattern requires the barrel be a true ESM barrel (not a CJS-style namespace bundler) and the side-effect module's top-level code be deterministic — anything that touches process.cwd() or other global state at the wrong time will move the wikiRoot capture order. Tests with chdir-in-before() should NOT add the side-effect import at file-top because that forces the wiki barrel load before chdir; instead, let the test's dynamic import of the wiki-tier module in before() pull in the barrel post-chdir.

records.text: When a wiki_context call returns "result exceeds maximum allowed tokens" and falls back to disk-saved chunked reading, the culprit is almost always one of: (1) omittedPageReasons growing unbounded as the wiki accumulates pages — each entry carries the full match-reason string ("content mentions X; claim text matches Y; ..."); 96 entries × ~350 chars = ~34k chars of payload that exists only as a triage hint. (2) Full handoff/memory/skill body text embedded in the briefing when the briefing is supposed to be a *briefing* — full bodies are available via memory_recall (by id) and wiki_skill_load. The fix is bounded caps in buildWikiContext: maxOmittedPageReasons (default 12) + maxOmittedReasonChars (default 80), maxHandoffTextChars (1200), maxMemoryTextChars (600), maxSkillTextChars (800), with a `truncateForBriefing(text, max)` helper that appends "…" so the agent knows it was clipped. Trade-off: agents must call wiki_skill_load / memory_recall for full bodies when the truncated body isn't enough — which is already the documented contract (the briefing already says "call wiki_skill_load(id) for full content"). Measured against the dendrite-wiki-mcp dogfood repo: caps reduced payload 91k → 38k chars (58%) without losing any actionable information. The reason this matters: the briefing is the single hottest tool surface (one call per session start by ritual), and an overweight briefing makes session resumption slow and expensive without making it more useful.

records.text: When adding a visual encoding channel to a force-directed graph that already has multiple visual signals, the right design move is to assign each channel a distinct temporal meaning rather than try to merge them — pulse glow for "right now," persistent outer ring for "in this window," static stroke for "current state." This works because operators reading the graph naturally chunk the visual signals by what they're attending to (latency vs context vs identity), so three layered channels stay legible even when they all fire on the same node. Concretely on the cortex view: the indigo drop-shadow pulse fires once per audit-log change (slice 2c.4), the amber outer ring renders while the change is inside the selected time window (slice 2c.5), and the kind-discriminated stroke marks the current supervision state (slice 2c.2). All three on one node tells the operator "this is an open-question that was just touched and is also touched within my browse window" — three independent facts conveyed without competing for the same visual real estate. The implementation discipline: use color hue as the discriminator (indigo vs amber vs kind-color), keep each channel monochrome within itself, and ensure each channel's update lifecycle (per-frame for pulse via rAF, computed-driven for ring, render-only for stroke) is independent so they never block each other.

records.text: When adding pan + zoom to a Vue + d3-force SVG visualization, the cleanest pattern is d3-zoom attached to the OUTER svg element with the zoom behavior writing its transform onto a reactive `viewportTransform` ref that drives an inner `<g class="viewport">` transform attribute. Wrap every visual element (edges, nodes, overlays) inside that viewport group so the entire graph moves as one unit; the encoding logic doesn't need to know about zoom at all. Three non-obvious details. (1) Use d3-zoom's `.filter()` callback to distinguish background-drag gestures from node-click gestures by checking `event.target.tagName` — return true for `svg`/`g` (background) and let everything else bubble through so per-node click handlers still fire. Without this, dragging anywhere starts a pan AND fires the click, opening drawers accidentally. (2) Some browsers fail to register the SVG element itself as the event target when a drag starts on truly empty pixels; add a `<rect>` at the top of the SVG with transparent fill, sized to the viewBox, as an explicit pan surface. (3) CSS `touch-action: none` on the SVG prevents mobile/trackpad gestures from triggering browser scroll while the user is panning the graph. The reset button uses `select(svgEl).transition().duration(400).call(zoomBehavior.transform, zoomIdentity)` for a smooth recovery from getting lost. Detach with `select(svgEl).on('.zoom', null)` in onBeforeUnmount so hot-reload doesn't leak listeners.

records.text: When auditing a codebase to extract a reusable library out of a single-product application, the right Phase 0 deliverable is a classification page committed to the wiki — every source file tagged as one of brain-pure / brain-leaky / wiki (or whatever the two-package split is) / shared / adapter, with explicit lists of hard couplings (where the to-be-extracted library calls back into the application layer) and vocabulary leaks (where the library's public types use application-specific terms). The reason this is a wiki page rather than a one-off scratch doc: the audit is the load-bearing reference for every subsequent phase, future readers (including future agents) need it for orientation, and committing it to the wiki means the existing drift-detection / contradicts-shipped-memory rules will flag it if the codebase moves and the audit doesn't. Practical method: grep `^import .* from '\./` across the to-be-extracted directory to map per-file local imports, then walk the resulting graph to find files that should be in the library but import from the application layer (those are the leaks). Vocabulary leaks are harder — search the library's type definitions for any string literal that mentions the application's name or domain (e.g., `'wiki'`, `'page'`, `'docs'`). Each leak gets a planned rename + a deprecation alias for one release cycle. Six-phase migration shape works: Phase 0 audit, Phase 1 storage adapter (lowest-risk plumbing first), Phase 2 abstract the cross-coupling, Phase 3 vocabulary rename, Phase 4 monorepo split, Phase 5 publish & dogfood the extracted library elsewhere. Each phase keeps `npm test` green at every commit; the second-project dogfood pass is the most important validation signal because it surfaces friction the in-product tests never could.

records.text: When d3-zoom .filter() needs to distinguish "interactive elements" from "background that should start a pan" in SVG, write the filter as a check for the interactive surface ONLY and let everything else pass — not as a whitelist of background tags. The interactive surface is a small enumerable set you can mark with a class (e.g. `target.closest('.node-group')`), the background surface is everything else and grows over time as you add new background elements (transparent rects, gradient stops, watermarks, focus rings). A whitelist-by-tagName filter like `tag === 'svg' || tag === 'g'` will silently break the moment anyone adds a `<rect>` pan-catcher or `<path>` decoration in the SVG. Concrete fix: `if (target.closest('.<interactive-class>')) return false; return true;`. This makes the filter robust against future SVG additions and matches how the same operator/agent reads the markup (interactive elements have semantic classes; background does not). Always keep `event.type === 'wheel'` as a separate true short-circuit at the top so wheel-zoom anywhere on the SVG keeps working even over interactive elements — d3-zoom's default wheel handler doesn't conflict with click handlers.

records.text: When migrating a second module to a storage adapter (Phase 1 slice 2 of a library extraction), the most useful design pivot is to reshape the adapter constructor from "one file path" to "one data directory" so future siblings share the same configured location. The reason this pivot has to happen now rather than later: every new persistent file the brain migrates would otherwise force the constructor signature to grow another path arg, and the adapter would carry N separate paths in its private state. Taking the data dir lets the adapter expose `get memoryStorePath()` / `get memoryEdgesPath()` getters that derive sibling paths consistently via `path.join`, and lets the env-var override (`DENDRITE_WIKI_DATA_DIR`) live in one place. Move write-strategy concerns (atomic tmp+rename, per-path in-process write queue, mkdir-on-write) INTO the adapter as implementation details — they're not brain concerns, they're storage concerns. Keep at the brain layer: schema migrations (normalizeStoredX), diff-friendly sorting (sort-by-id before write), and any domain-specific normalization that must happen before bytes hit disk. Test the adapter's atomicity contract directly: fire two concurrent writes via Promise.all and assert (a) both succeed without throwing, (b) the resulting file is valid JSON not corrupted, and (c) no .tmp.* stragglers remain in the data directory. Backward-compat trick: when reshaping a constructor used in slice 1's tests, just update that one test in the same PR — slice 1's adapter API is still new enough that its consumers are only within this repo, so the breaking change is contained.

records.text: When migrating a sync-fs module to an async storage adapter as part of a library extraction phase, the cascade is usually tractable rather than painful because every modern call path is already async — the sync persistence was historical inertia, not a deliberate choice for hot-path latency. Practical sequence: (1) flip the module's read/write helpers async first; (2) flip the module's public functions async; (3) for in-process call sites already inside async functions, return Promise propagates naturally — `return wrapToolResponse(...)` from an async handler keeps working when wrapToolResponse's return type changes from `T` to `Promise<T>` because the async function already returns Promise<T>; (4) for fire-and-forget contexts where the persist outcome is independent of the response (e.g., a tool-rejection path that records an attempt for telemetry), use `void someAsyncCall()` instead of awaiting so the caller doesn't pay the latency; (5) external hook scripts under .claude/hooks/*.mjs that need sync semantics (PreToolUse / Stop blockers) stay sync via `readFileSync` inline — they don't go through the in-process async adapter, which is the right architectural boundary because hook scripts run in a separate process anyway. Test pattern: rewrite every existing test()'s callback to async + add await before every now-async call; the diff is mechanical and the test suite catches any missed call site immediately. The async-everywhere flip is a one-time cost for the whole phase rather than a per-slice cost — once ritual-state flipped, all downstream brain modules are uniformly async.

records.text: When migrating an append-only JSONL stream to a storage adapter (Phase 1 slice 3 of a library extraction), the API shape that matters most is the targeted appendObservationLine method, NOT the coarse read-whole / write-whole pattern. The reason this design call differs from previous slices: every-tool-call append paths can be 50x or more more I/O if forced through read-whole/write-whole at the file's line cap — observations:capture fires on every Edit/Write/Read/Bash, so a 5000-line file rewrite on each call would dominate the per-tool-call latency budget. Targeted append via fs.appendFile is O(1) per call and naturally atomic for sub-PIPE_BUF payloads (POSIX O_APPEND guarantees no torn writes for small payloads, which a single JSON observation line comfortably is). The interface still grows a writeObservationLines coarse method because the lazy retention pass (trim to last N) genuinely needs the read-whole / pick-tail / write-whole pattern. Three-method shape: appendObservationLine (single line including trailing newline), readObservationLines (returns string[] of non-empty lines), writeObservationLines (rewrite with given lines, joined and trailing-newline-terminated so the next append lands cleanly). Test the compose pattern that models actual usage — append N, trim to K, then append one more — and assert the new append lands as line K+1 not line K (which would happen if writeObservationLines forgot the trailing newline). Single source of truth for the path resolver stays in memory-storage.ts; the original module exports a legacy alias.

records.text: When operator-supplied content needs to flow from the maintenance review board UI into a backend action, do NOT add a generic argumentOverrides map to the bridge — add a narrow typed scalar (or small typed object) for that one action's needs. Pattern: bridge POST body grows one new optional field (e.g., summaryDraft: string), runMaintenanceActionAndRefresh accepts it via RunMaintenanceActionOptions, executeMaintenanceAction pipes it through ExecuteMaintenanceActionOptions, and only the specific action's switch case consults it. Other actions ignore the field. Reasoning: a general overrides map would let an operator rewrite slug/path/days/etc. on any action, opening a path-traversal-style risk. The narrow field is impossible to misuse against unrelated actions because they don't read it. Vue side: runActionViaBridge took an argumentOverrides param shaped like { newFirstParagraph?: string } and serialized it as summaryDraft on the wire — keeps the frontend caller-friendly while the wire format stays narrow. Confirmation gate must mirror in TWO places: requiresBridgeConfirmation in src/wiki/review-bridge.ts AND actionNeedsConfirmation in MaintenanceReviewBoard.vue. They will silently disagree if you only update one (bridge 409s, UI doesn't show window.confirm), so always grep both when adding a new high-risk action kind.

records.text: When refactoring filesystem access in an existing module behind a new adapter interface (Phase 1 of a library extraction), the cheapest path that keeps tests green is "indirection without API change": (1) define the adapter interface and its filesystem implementation in a new module; (2) refactor the existing read/write helpers in the original module to construct an adapter per-call and delegate — preserves the legacy `root: string = process.cwd()` parameter pattern across every public function; (3) extract any path-resolution constant to the new module as the single source of truth, then re-export the legacy resolver name from the original module as a thin pass-through so existing callers (tests, other src modules, generated API reference pages) keep working without code churn. The reason this minimizes risk: the public API of the original module is unchanged, the test suite needs no edits, and the only diff is the internal delegation. Type-only imports avoid runtime circular dependencies when the adapter module needs the original module's schema types — `import type { Foo } from './original.js'` gets erased at compile time so the runtime import graph stays acyclic. Promote internal types to public exports when the adapter needs them. Test the adapter in isolation (round-trip, missing file, whitespace-only file, mkdir-on-write, env-var-driven path overrides, direct-path construction) — pins the contract independently of the original module's normalization passes. Don't add a module-level singleton adapter in this phase; future phases lift the indirection into the public surface once a second backend exists to motivate the API change.

records.text: When the operator asks you to "organize the wiki", "run the librarian", or words to that effect, the canonical workflow is: (1) call `wiki_librarian_audit` ONCE — it returns every open maintenance signal with pre-gathered evidence and a `recommendedAction` sentence per item, so you don't need to chain wiki_maintenance_inbox + memory_review + lintWikiPages + per-page reads yourself; (2) work down the items in the order the audit returns them (promotion-ready first because it's the most deterministic, then contradicts-shipped-memory, then page-drift, then claims/orphan lint); (3) execute each `recommendedAction` using the named tools — typically memory_promote with mode="apply" for promotions, wiki_read + wiki_write for prose rewrites; (4) for page-drift findings on roadmap/tracker-style pages, expect that even an accurate rewrite leaves Jaccard overlap below the 35% threshold because the project log inherently uses more concrete shipping vocabulary than any prose can match — the right close-out is to snooze via `wiki_execute_maintenance_action` with actionId `lint:page-drift:docs/wiki/<slug>.md:snooze-page-drift` (30-day quiet period); (5) at the end, summarize what changed so the operator can `git diff` the actual edits without rereading the audit. Every change still writes through the audited path (project-log entry + memory supersede + reversible file mutation), so the operator's safety story stays identical to manual editing.

records.text: When wrapping a multi-slice library-extraction phase, the closing slice should mirror Phase 1's pattern exactly: (1) audit the modules the phase was supposed to migrate to confirm no residual coupling, (2) extract any small remaining call sites into the adapter interface — even one extra method is fine if it's the only thing left, (3) write a per-phase contract test in test/brain-no-X-coupling.test.ts that asserts the named modules do not import from the named source, with a clear remediation message pointing at the adapter. The per-phase test pattern is the difference between "phase done for now" and "phase done and stays done." Naming: brain-no-direct-fs.test.ts (Phase 1), brain-no-wiki-coupling.test.ts (Phase 2), future phases get similar names. Each contract test is paired with a sanity check that the ADAPTER itself still has the coupling that's the whole point — if a future edit accidentally removes the adapter's wiki/fs import, the contract is silently broken because no migrated module is allowed to bypass anymore, and the sanity check catches that exact failure mode. The contract assertions are also living documentation: an outsider reading test/brain-no-*-coupling.test.ts files learns which modules went through which phase and why.

records.text: When wrapping a multi-slice refactor phase, the closing slice should include a contract assertion test that pins the property the phase was designed to deliver. For a storage-adapter library extraction, the contract is "the migrated brain modules must not regress to direct fs imports." Implementation: a simple TypeScript test that walks the canonical module list, reads each file, regex-searches for `from ['"]node:fs(?:\/promises)?['"]/` , and fails with a clear remediation message naming the adapter to use instead. Scope the assertion narrowly to the modules the phase actually migrated, NOT the whole directory — the broader assertion catches modules with legitimate fs use (caches, exports, snapshots, benchmark history) that were intentionally outside the phase's scope, and forces a maintenance battle every time someone adds a legitimate fs caller. The scoped list also serves as living documentation of what the phase accomplished. Pair the primary assertion with a sanity-check that the adapter ITSELF still imports fs — guards against an edit accidentally removing the adapter's fs import, which would silently break the entire contract because no migrated module is allowed to bypass anymore. The contract test is the difference between "Phase 1 is done for now" and "Phase 1 is done and stays done across every future commit."

records.text: When you need to decorate VitePress sidebar or nav links with dynamic per-link metadata (counts, badges, status indicators) and the default theme has no slot for it, the pragmatic pattern is a DOM-side-effect-only Vue component mounted in #layout-bottom: (1) component template renders nothing visible (`<span style="display:none">`) — its only job is mount lifecycle; (2) on mount, fetch the data and run a `decorate()` function that does `document.querySelectorAll('.VPSidebar a[href*="/wiki/"], .VPNav a[href*="/wiki/"], .VPLocalNav a[href*="/wiki/"]')` and appends a child span with the badge to each matching anchor; (3) a MutationObserver on document.body with childList+subtree fires `decorateSoon()` on debounce (~80ms) to handle VitePress re-rendering the sidebar on route change or expand/collapse — the debounce is essential because expanding a section fires many mutations in rapid succession and re-querying the whole sidebar on each one is wasteful; (4) styles for the injected class MUST be in a non-scoped `<style>` block (the global CSS) because the injected nodes live inside sibling components' DOM trees and scoped styles wouldn't apply to them. Use `pointer-events: none` on the badge so clicks pass through to the parent anchor. Forward-compat selector strategy: prefer specific VitePress classes but include `[href*="/wiki/"]` as a fallback so the decorator keeps working if VitePress renames a class in a future release.
